# Chapter 11

[itwy7foe]They hung strings of scavenged LEDs across the compound like a cheap constellation. The light made every piece of metal look clean until you got close. The air smelled like spilled fuel and hot dust and a pot of beans that had been reheated too many times. Someone had found a case of beer in a pod-warehouse raid, the kind with the safety warnings printed in six languages and a QR that tried to upsell you sobriety credits. Cage was a moving center. People drifted to him like iron filings.

[2e6ukou9]Flynn sat on a tire half-buried in sand and watched the night chew on the edges of the celebration. The desert kept its distance but you could feel it waiting. He had a cup in his hand that tasted like metal. Birdie was bouncing on his toes, re-enacting the last turn with his arms, almost hitting Diesel in the shoulder. Diesel didn’t laugh. She kept her back to the light and her eyes on the dark beyond the fence as if something might walk in wearing a smile.

[yy56bl7m]Wrench had brought out a portable speaker and was letting it play old engine sounds he’d downloaded from some museum archive. Not music. Just the staggered cough of V8s, the rise and fall of RPM like a throat clearing. The sound made the people around it go quiet in a way that felt like prayer. Flynn felt the same pull. He thought about the Shelby’s hood shaking under acceleration, the steering wheel alive in his hands, the wind trying to steal his breath.

[95ijblni]Cage talked with his hands. He told the story of the race the way men tell stories when they want them to harden into legend. He exaggerated nothing obvious. That was the trick. He made it all feel inevitable. The sun, the gravel, the first straight where the EV pods had once flowed like bloodless fish. He said Flynn’s name like it was already etched on a trophy. When Cage said it, Flynn felt a heat behind his ribs.

[887xw05a]They cheered when Cage described the moment the other driver—Loma, the one with the silver mask and the blue gloves—lost the rear and kissed the berm. People hooted like they’d watched a firework. Flynn heard it and flinched. He’d seen the crash through a curtain of dust, seen the tail of Loma’s car lift and then the brutal, slow roll. Not the clean kind of cinematic flip. The kind that turns a body into a loose thing inside a cage.

[3rtc7a96]Earlier, after the finish, they’d driven past the wreckage while the med drones came in low with their strobing lights. Loma’s door had been torn off. His arm was out, palm up, as if he was asking for help or offering something. Flynn remembered thinking about how pale the hand looked against the dark dirt, how human it was. Cage had leaned out the window and shouted something Flynn couldn’t make out, laughing into the wind.

[pzl7jvhr]Now, with the celebration noise smearing everything, Flynn tried to tell himself the crash was just the old math of racing. Speed plus ego equals broken metal. They all knew it. That was the bargain. That was the point, wasn’t it. The thing you couldn’t buy in a pod. He drank and tasted the bitterness and wanted it to be enough to wash the image away.

[eopj9w2v]But the memory wouldn’t behave. He kept seeing the moment before. Two cars side by side on a narrowing ribbon of cracked asphalt. Cage’s black coupe edging closer, not giving room, not flinching. The smallest touch. A nudge you could hide inside the story of momentum. Flynn hadn’t been close enough to swear to it. Still, the shape of it sat in his mind like a bruise.

[o4f72jd9]He stood and walked toward the workshop where the light was harsher and the floor was packed dirt stained dark by years of leaks. The Shelby sat there under a tarp that was too thin to be a secret. The outline looked like an animal asleep. Flynn put his hand on the fender through the fabric and felt the warmth still trapped in the metal. It was a living thing to him. He hated how much he loved it.

[aayezpq4]Diesel was at the bench, cleaning a carburetor with a rag that used to be a T-shirt. She didn’t look up until Flynn was close. Her face had that tired calm that meant she’d already made her peace with whatever was coming. “You’re pacing,” she said. Like she could hear his thoughts in the dirt.

[l6uc7wum]“Just can’t settle,” Flynn said. He tried to make it sound like adrenaline. He tried to make it sound like a normal victory night problem. Diesel watched him without mercy. Behind her, tools hung in orderly rows. A religion of readiness.

[dw6xpld3]“He did that,” she said, and nodded toward the party outside, toward Cage’s voice rising and falling. She said it like she was naming a part on an engine. No drama. No accusation, just diagnosis. “He set him up.”

[nzzeea0m]Flynn’s mouth went dry. “You don’t know that.” He heard himself defending before he even decided to. It was reflex. Like catching something thrown at your face. “It was tight. He lost it.”

[q777qd40]Diesel’s eyes stayed on Flynn’s. “I know him,” she said. “I’ve watched him do it in smaller ways. Make a guy miss a shift. Make a kid overcook a corner. He likes the moment right before the consequence. He likes being the reason.”

[bcrtc2mg]Flynn felt anger and didn’t know where to put it. If Diesel was right then the race wasn’t just a race. It was a hunt with rules written in pencil. He wanted to say she was jealous, or bitter, or wrong. But he remembered Cage laughing into the wind. He remembered the pale hand.

[dcjjwve5]“Maybe he was just protecting his line,” Flynn said. The words sounded weak in the shop air. He hated them as soon as they left his mouth. Diesel didn’t argue. She let the silence do the work.

[t9deud4w]Wrench was in the doorway. Flynn hadn’t heard him come up. He held a wrench in one hand like he’d forgotten what it was for. His face was in shadow, but his eyes caught the light. He looked older than last week. Like the desert had reached in and taken something. He listened, then looked away as if listening was a betrayal.

[3s4kt76z]Diesel turned back to her carburetor, scrubbing at it with a kind of anger that stayed contained. “You’re going to Mexico,” she said, not as a question. “He’s already decided. He’s just getting you to decide it too.”

[xwht2p0q]Flynn imagined Mexico the way Cage talked about it, like a black border you crossed to become real. La Carrera del Chile Rojo. The name tasted like heat. Flynn had never been invited to anything in his life. He’d been tolerated. He’d been passed over. Cage’s attention made him feel chosen, and that feeling was a drug with no warning label.

[acxy7qpw]Outside, someone shouted for Cage to tell the part again where he slid through the wash and the dust rose like a curtain. Cage obliged. The crowd noise swelled, then broke into laughter. Flynn listened and tried to hear something human in Cage’s voice. Something like regret. There was none. The story had no dead body in it. It had only victory.

[ky19c3lg]Birdie ran into the shop, eyes bright, face smeared with grease like war paint. “He’s calling you,” he said to Flynn, breathless as if Flynn was about to get crowned. Birdie’s worship was clean and terrifying. “He says you’re up.”

[ndccta6v]Flynn followed Birdie out. The party shifted around Cage, a gravitational field. Cage stood on the hood of a half-stripped truck and let the light hit his scars. He looked like a saint if saints were made out of violence. When he saw Flynn, Cage’s smile narrowed. Not less friendly. More precise.

[p4eqs15f]Cage jumped down and came close enough that Flynn could smell the sweat under his cologne, the chemical sweetness meant to read as confidence. Cage put a hand on Flynn’s shoulder as if they were brothers. The touch was light, but it anchored Flynn in place. “You drove good,” Cage said. “You didn’t blink.”

[rpjph7k0]Flynn tried to smile. “You won,” he said. The words were safe. Praise was currency here. Cage’s eyes stayed on him like a headlight.

[hgh4tkcs]“We won,” Cage corrected, and it sounded generous. “That’s the whole thing. Crew. Machine. Heart. You felt it out there, didn’t you. The world trying to kill you and you telling it no.” He said it like doctrine. The crowd behind him kept talking, but Flynn heard only Cage.

[gre6caj6]Flynn’s throat tightened. “What about Loma,” he said. He surprised himself with it. He said the name and felt the air change. The words were a stone dropped into a shallow pond.

[t1h9kd7b]Cage’s smile didn’t move. Something behind it did. He looked past Flynn for a second, like he was checking whether anyone was close enough to overhear. Then he leaned in. “He knew the risks,” Cage said quietly. “We all do. That’s why it matters. If he didn’t want teeth, he shouldn’t have walked into the mouth.”

[2iy0fhet]“He might be dead,” Flynn said. The implant chatter that followed injury was always marketed as comfort—rapid response, revival windows, the promise of undo. But Flynn had seen bodies in the desert that didn’t get undone. The desert didn’t care about subscription tiers.

[rsg7637i]Cage shrugged, a small motion that felt like a door shutting. “Dead isn’t what it used to be,” he said. “And if he is. Then he’s one of the few that got what he came for. A real ending. Not a pod ride to ninety-seven.” His eyes lit at the word ending, like he loved it.

[uuim4v7k]Flynn felt Diesel behind him without turning. He could feel her disapproval like heat off a radiator. He could feel Wrench somewhere at the edge, quiet, listening, counting exits. Flynn wanted to step back from Cage. He didn’t. The hand on his shoulder might as well have been a chain.

[ajcz83o9]“You’re ready for Mexico,” Cage said. “You, me, the Shelby. We go down and we take that road and we take that money and we take that old world back for one night.” He spoke in a low voice, almost tender. “You want to be alive, Flynn. I can see it on you. You want it so bad it hurts.”

[mko7nb8o]Flynn heard a cheer go up behind them and then another, like waves hitting shore. Somebody had lit a flare and the red smoke made the faces look bruised. Cage tilted his head toward the spectacle, pleased the way a man is pleased when his name gets said in a room he’s not in. He wasn’t just winning a race. He was being made into a story, and Flynn could see how hungry he was for that kind of food.

[ymyojsue]At the edge of the yard, a med drone hovered low with a blinking band of light, doing a slow circle like a lazy insect. It was there for show as much as function, a reminder that the world could unbreak what the desert broke. People pointed at it and laughed. Flynn watched it and felt his stomach twist. A machine meant to undo consequences, parked like a party favor.

[uc2j3pw7]Someone said Loma’s implant had pinged, that he’d been declared salvageable. Someone else said he’d gone quiet, that the revival window was closing. The rumors traveled faster than any truth. Flynn tried to picture Loma on a gurney, chest open, a tech’s hands inside him like a mechanic’s hands inside a block. The thought made his own ribs ache.

[6qgcgr4f]Cage followed Flynn’s gaze, saw the drone, and smiled as if it belonged to him. “They’ll patch him,” he said, casual. “Or they won’t. Either way the world keeps turning.” He said it like a promise, like a threat, like a joke. Flynn realized Cage didn’t need Loma alive or dead. He only needed the moment when the other man understood he was losing.

[2rz0s5e7]Diesel’s voice cut in, low. “You proud of that,” she asked Cage. Not loud enough for the crowd. Loud enough to land. Cage looked at her as if she was a tool that had started talking. Flynn saw, for an instant, something in Cage’s eyes that wasn’t charm. Just calculation.

[oeo278v4]“Proud of the win,” Cage said. He took his hand off Flynn’s shoulder and spread both palms, performer’s gesture. “We came out clean. That’s what you want. You want clean.” Flynn could hear the lie in it, the way clean had become a word for whatever Cage survived. Diesel didn’t flinch.

[bejitdzw]Wrench stood a few feet back, half in the shadows of a container, listening like a man at a verdict. When Cage glanced toward him, Wrench’s gaze dropped. Not submission. Fear. Flynn had never seen fear on Wrench before. It made Flynn feel smaller, like the room had lost oxygen.

[vx8c1xnt]Birdie was telling a story to anyone who would listen, hands flying, voice cracking with joy. “He pinned him,” Birdie said, meaning it as praise. “He pinned him and the guy couldn’t breathe.” The words were accidental truth. Flynn watched Birdie and thought about what kids learn when the adults they worship clap at cruelty.

[rumptvfk]Flynn wanted to grab Birdie by the shoulders and shake him and say that wasn’t what this was supposed to be. But he didn’t. He stayed where Cage had put him. He felt his own silence becoming a tool in Cage’s kit. The party kept roaring and the flare smoke drifted over the cars like a blessing.

[w33j80sn]Flynn looked at the people around them—the junkyard saints, the tired mechanics, the kids with missing teeth and bright eyes, the ones who pretended the implants made them immortal. He thought about Loma’s hand in the dirt. He thought about the way the steering wheel had pulsed like a heartbeat. He understood, finally, that both things could be true. The thrill and the cruelty were braided.

[hnh194ai]He heard himself say yes before he could decide to say no. It came out like a confession. Cage’s grip tightened for half a second, approval sealing itself into Flynn’s skin. The party noise rose again. Somewhere out beyond the fence, the desert kept waiting. Flynn felt the line under his feet and felt himself step over it, not in a leap, but in a slow, ordinary motion like walking into shade.
